title: Quantum Markovian Dynamics from a Double Covariance Stochastic Framework
abstract: We develop an interacting extension of the Double Covariance Model (DCM), a stochastic subquantum framework in which macroscopic quantum dynamics emerge through coarse-graining of correlated microscopic fluctuations. Starting from local stochastic differential equations on subsystem Hilbert spaces, we derive a closed evolution equation for a coarse-grained double covariance operator using multi-scale Itô calculus and sliding-window averaging. The construction explicitly incorporates two separated temporal scales: a fast microscopic fluctuation scale governing subquantum stochastic processes and a slower macroscopic observation scale associated with coarse-grained dynamics. Within the hydrodynamic limit, where the ratio between microscopic correlation time and averaging-window scale vanishes, rapidly fluctuating corrections disappear and the effective dynamics converges to a deterministic macroscopic transport equation. We show that the emergent macroscopic dynamics has the exact Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad (GKSL) form: coherent Hamiltonian evolution arises from deterministic subquantum flow, while dissipative channels emerge from quadratic noise correlations. The framework further demonstrates how non-separable interaction Hamiltonians can arise from strictly local, state-dependent stochastic feedback fields. In the fluctuation-free limit, the model reduces naturally to the standard von Neumann equation, providing a unified stochastic foundation for both open and closed quantum dynamics.
summary: This paper develops the Double Covariance Model (DCM) to derive quantum dynamics from classical stochastic processes. By employing multi-scale calculus and coarse-graining, the authors show that the standard GKSL master equation emerges naturally as the effective macroscopic equation. This framework unifies quantum theory by showing the von Neumann equation is a limiting case of the general stochastic evolution.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive the standard, Markovian evolution equations of quantum mechanics (like the GKSL equation) from a more fundamental, underlying classical stochastic process.
main_result: The Double Covariance Model successfully reproduces the GKSL master equation in the macroscopic limit, while the closed-system von Neumann equation emerges as the fluctuation-free limit.
method: It employs multi-scale Itô calculus and a hydrodynamic coarse-graining procedure, averaging over fast microscopic fluctuations to derive the effective macroscopic dynamics.
model_or_system: The model uses the Double Covariance Model (DCM), treating quantum states as macroscopic covariance operators derived from complex-valued stochastic processes. The dynamics are governed by a coarse-grained evolution equation that yields the GKSL form.
key_observables: The normalized double covariance operator $C(	au)$ and the resulting density operator $ho(	au)$.
important_parameters: The scale separation parameter $\epsilon$, which governs the transition from fast microscopic fluctuations ($\delta t = O(\epsilon^2)$) to slow macroscopic observation ($\Delta = O(\epsilon)$).
main_assumptions: The core assumptions include the existence of two separated temporal scales and the assumption of white-noise (delta-correlated) microscopic noise to enforce Markovianity.
paper_structure: The structure follows a derivation path: defining the microscopic stochastic process, applying multi-scale Itô calculus to find the evolution of the covariance tensor, and finally performing the hydrodynamic coarse-graining to yield the macroscopic GKSL equation.
why_interesting: This work provides a deep, non-standard foundation for open quantum systems, suggesting that the GKSL structure is an emergent statistical consequence of underlying classical noise, rather than a postulate.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
